risks.title: AiTM credential phishing leading to M365/Entra account takeover
risks.threat_source: External financially motivated phishing crews using adversary-in-the-middle kits (Evilginx-class) that proxy the real Entra login page and steal session cookies, defeating Authenticator push MFA.
risks.asset: Employee Entra ID identities and everything behind them: Exchange Online mail, SharePoint/OneDrive tenant data, and the ~15 downstream SSO SaaS apps, including any admin roles they carry.
risks.exposure: 200 remote employees; MFA is phishable push with no Conditional Access device-compliance or authentication-strength policies; a 3-person IT team with no SIEM cannot triage sign-in anomalies at scale.
risks.scenario: An employee authenticates through a proxied login page and approves the push; the attacker replays the session cookie, sets inbox rules, commits vendor-payment fraud, and bulk-downloads customer data via SharePoint and SSO-connected apps. Distinct from R4: the compromise is of the cloud identity/session, not the endpoint itself.

risks.title: Public web app exploitation (SQLi/IDOR/CVE) leading to PostgreSQL customer-data exfiltration
risks.threat_source: External opportunistic mass scanners and targeted attackers exploiting OWASP-class flaws (SQL injection, IDOR, SSRF) or unpatched framework/library CVEs in the internet-facing multi-tenant app.
risks.asset: Business records and PII of ~500k end users across ~1,200 customer orgs in Azure Database for PostgreSQL, plus the app-layer tenant-isolation boundary.
risks.exposure: Internet-facing App Service with no WAF; no dedicated security engineer to drive secure-SDLC gates or rapid patching; a single application flaw can cross tenant boundaries in the shared database.
risks.scenario: A scanner finds an injectable endpoint or known CVE; the attacker dumps tenant tables through the application's database access and extorts with a published sample, triggering breach notification to ~1,200 customer orgs. Distinct from R3: entry is via a flaw in the app itself, not stolen credentials or pipeline secrets.

risks.title: Leaked CI/CD and cloud secrets leading to Azure production subscription compromise
risks.threat_source: External attackers harvesting live secrets from repository history, infostealer-compromised developer laptops (GitHub PATs, cached tokens), or malicious/compromised GitHub Actions dependencies.
risks.asset: The single production Azure subscription: App Service, PostgreSQL connection credentials, Blob Storage including backups, and the GitHub Actions pipeline that deploys to it.
risks.exposure: Historical .env files and connection strings committed to private repos; long-lived service-principal secrets with Contributor rights in GitHub Actions; ~60 engineers whose access multiplies leak paths; no dedicated security staff monitoring for secret exposure.
risks.scenario: An infostealer on a dev laptop lifts a GitHub PAT; the attacker mines repo history for connection strings, authenticates as the Contributor-level CI service principal, exfiltrates the database and backups, and plants persistence. Distinct from R5: the credentials are stolen by an outsider, not retained by a departed insider.

risks.title: Ransomware/extortion via unmanaged remote endpoints
risks.threat_source: Ransomware affiliates and initial-access brokers delivering infostealers and loaders through malvertising, trojanized installers, and commodity phishing aimed at remote workers' laptops.
risks.asset: Employee laptops, cached corporate credentials and Entra tokens, locally synced OneDrive/SharePoint data, and continuity of support, billing, and engineering operations.
risks.exposure: ~40% of laptops not Intune-enrolled; users commonly local admins; Defender AV on defaults with no EDR and no SIEM, so nobody is watching alerts; remote-first workforce mixes personal use with work devices.
risks.scenario: A trojanized installer on an unenrolled laptop escalates via local admin, steals tokens, and encrypts synced SharePoint libraries through the OneDrive client; the 3-person IT team faces multi-day recovery and an extortion demand. Distinct from R1: the foothold is malware on the device, not a phished cloud session.

risks.title: Offboarding gaps: dormant employee/contractor access to production data
risks.threat_source: Departed or departing employees and contractors retaining valid credentials (malicious or negligent), and external attackers credential-stuffing dormant accounts that no one is watching.
risks.asset: Production PostgreSQL (direct contractor database accounts), GitHub source code, and customer data reachable via lingering M365/Entra and downstream SaaS access; credibility of the in-progress SOC 2 program.
risks.exposure: Offboarding is manual and ticket-driven across Entra, ~15 SSO apps, Azure, GitHub, and direct Postgres accounts; contractors hold standing database logins outside SSO, so a missed ticket leaves working production access indefinitely.
risks.scenario: A contractor's engagement ends but their Postgres login survives for months; after a payment dispute they export customer tables — or an attacker reuses the dormant credential — with no detection either way. Distinct from R3: access here was legitimately granted and never revoked, not exfiltrated.

validation_notes: R2 draft asserted facts not in the profile: a single high-privilege app DB role, no security code review/DAST gate, and multi-week patch latency. These are plausible for a team with no security engineer but are assumptions — removed from the entry; confirm the app's actual DB role privileges and patch cadence.

R3 draft asserted 'no secret scanning or rotation' and 'Key Vault used inconsistently' — neither is in the profile. Removed; verify whether GitHub secret scanning / push protection and Key Vault are actually in use before scoring likelihood.

R5 draft asserted shared service accounts with non-rotated passwords and no periodic access recertification. The profile confirms manual offboarding and direct contractor DB accounts but is silent on shared accounts and recertification — removed as facts; both are worth confirming in fieldwork.

Distinctness check passed with sharpened boundaries: R1 (phished cloud identity/session) vs R4 (endpoint malware); R2 (app-layer flaw) vs R3 (stolen secrets/pipeline) vs R5 (retained legitimate access). Each entry now states its boundary explicitly in the scenario field.
All five entries kept their original ids and core subjects; only specificity, unsupported facts, and overlap boundaries were changed.
coverage_gaps: Backup integrity and recoverability: Blob Storage backups live in the same single Azure subscription with no stated immutability, isolation, or restore testing — a subscription-level compromise (R3) or ransomware event could destroy backups along with production, and RTO/RPO are unvalidated.

Third-party SaaS vendor breach: a compromise at one of the ~15 downstream SSO apps (or a data processor holding synced customer data) could expose Meridian Desk customer PII with no register entry, monitoring, or vendor-risk process covering it.

Insider/engineer overexposure to production data: ~60 engineers plus contractors can reach production systems with no SIEM or database audit logging, so deliberate or accidental bulk access by a current (not departed) insider would be invisible — adjacent to but not covered by R5.
